name: Kikuyu
description: The Agikuyu are the largest ethnic group in Kenya, inhabiting the fertile highlands of Central Kenya around Mount Kenya — which they call Kiriinyaga. Their cosmology centres on Ngai, the Supreme God who dwells on the mountain's peak. Their society is organised into age-sets (mariika) and councils of elders (kiama). The fig tree (mugumo) is sacred, serving as a site of prayer and sacrifice. Traditionally farmers and traders, the Kikuyu played a central role in Kenya's independence movement.
cultural_highlights: Age-set (mariika) system organises civic duties, rites of passage, and governance from circumcision to elderhood
Thigi tree council — elders hold court under the mugumo fig tree for dispute resolution
Gichandi — a traditional rattle instrument played alongside oral poetry competitions
Mũthirigu — a banned colonial-era protest dance that embedded coded political messages
Gũtũra ceremony — nine-bead beadwork symbolising life stages from birth to death

food_traditions.name: Mũrũo (Githeri)
food_traditions.description: Slow-cooked maize and beans, the foundational everyday meal

food_traditions.name: Mũtũra
food_traditions.description: Stuffed intestine — a delicacy at celebrations and harvest festivals

food_traditions.name: Irio
food_traditions.description: Mashed green peas, potato and maize — a comfort staple associated with Central Kenya highlands

food_traditions.name: Njohi ya mũgũnde
food_traditions.description: Fermented sugarcane beer used in ritual and social gatherings

music_traditions: Gichandi rattle-poetry — competitive performance art combining percussion and oral literature
Mwomboko — a communal circle dance with call-and-response singing at harvest
Kamba-influence benga — fusion guitar popular in Kikuyu urban settings
